coredata.dc:title: Highly sensitive fluoroprobe for detecting Sudan dyes in paprika utilizing carbon dot-embedded zeolitic imidazolate framework-8
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yanjie
Wei, Xiaofeng
Su, Yu
Xu, Rui
Song, Daqian
Ding, Lan
Chen, Yanhua
coredata.dc:description: In this manuscript, we synthesized CDs@ZIF-8 through a one-step, in-situ method by integrating green-emitting carbon dots (CDs) with zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8). The resulting CDs@ZIF-8 was utilized as an ultrasensitive probe for detection, leveraging the inner filter effect. The analysis demonstrated the capability to detect Sudan dyes. Sudan I, for example, could be detected within a concentration range spanning from 0.25 to 70 μM, achieving a remarkable detection limit of 76.56 nM. This established method was effectively employed for detecting Sudan I in paprika. Compared with CDs, CDs@ZIF-8 exhibited a 3.32-fold increase in sensitivity and a wider detection range. This enhanced performance was attributed to the porous ZIF-8, which allowed for the enrichment of targets around CDs and avoided the aggregation of CDs. Additionally, embedding the CDs in ZIF-8 improved their pH stability. Our study provides a new approach for using CDs under limited conditions by leveraging metal–organic frameworks.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8)
Sudan dyes
Fluorescence detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Sudan dyes with CDs@ZIF-8
Detection of Sudan I in actual samples
Optimization for the synthesis of CDs@ZIF-8

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 201804049), Jilin Provincial Science and Technology Development Plan Project (20230402022GH), and The Open Funds of the State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry (SKLEAC202305).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of CDs@ZIF-8 for Sudan I detection.
TEM images: (A) CDs with inset showing the distribution of CD diameters; (B) ZIF-8 with inset showing a single ZIF-8 particle; (C) and (D) TEM images of CDs@ZIF-8 at different scales.

(A) FT-IR spectra of CDs, ZIF-8, and CDs@ZIF-8. (B) XRD patterns of the prepared ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8. (C) TGA curves of ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8 under N2（inset: partial enlargement). (D) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8 (E) and corresponding pore-size distribution curves (F).

(A) UV–vis spectra of CDs@ZIF-8, Sudan Ⅰ, CDs@ZIF-8＋Sudan Ⅰ. (B) Normalized UV–vis spectrum of Sudan Ⅰ (black), excitation (blue), and emission (red) spectra of CDs@ZIF-8. (C) The fluorescence lifetime of CDs@ZIF-8 in the absence and presence of 10 μM Sudan Ⅰ. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs@ZIF-8 in the presence of various concentrations of Sudan I in a paprika sample. (B)The relationships between F0/F and Sudan I concentrations in a paprika sample (red) and pure solution (blue). (F0: the fluorescence intensity of CDs@ZIF-8, F: the fluorescence intensity in the presence of Sudan I). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Quantification of Sudan I in paprika using CDs@ZIF-8 as fluorescent probe. (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Highly sensitive fluoroprobe for detecting Sudan dyes in paprika utilizing carbon dot-embedded zeolitic imidazolate framework-8

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, 2699 Qianjin Street, Changchun 130012, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, 2699 Qianjin Street, Changchun 130012, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jilin Province Research Center for Engineering and Technology of Spectral Analytical Instruments, Jilin University, 2699 Qianjin Street, Changchun 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jilin Province Research Center for Engineering and Technology of Spectral Analytical Instruments

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jilin Province Research Center for Engineering and Technology of Spectral Analytical Instruments, Jilin University, 2699 Qianjin Street, Changchun 130012, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Yanjie Wang and Xiaofeng Wei should be considered joint first author.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this manuscript, we synthesized CDs@ZIF-8 through a one-step, in-situ method by integrating green-emitting carbon dots (CDs) with zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8). The resulting CDs@ZIF-8 was utilized as an ultrasensitive probe for detection, leveraging the inner filter effect. The analysis demonstrated the capability to detect Sudan dyes. Sudan I, for example, could be detected within a concentration range spanning from 0.25 to 70 μM, achieving a remarkable detection limit of 76.56 nM. This established method was effectively employed for detecting Sudan I in paprika. Compared with CDs, CDs@ZIF-8 exhibited a 3.32-fold increase in sensitivity and a wider detection range. This enhanced performance was attributed to the porous ZIF-8, which allowed for the enrichment of targets around CDs and avoided the aggregation of CDs. Additionally, embedding the CDs in ZIF-8 improved their pH stability. Our study provides a new approach for using CDs under limited conditions by leveraging metal–organic frameworks.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Sudan dyes are synthetic-azo dyes known for their fat-solubility and widespread use in various industries, such as textiles, shoes, hydrocarbon solvents, cosmetics, and various industrial fields (Yao-Say Solomon Adade et al., 2022; Yu, Lee, Liu, & Yang, 2019). However, due to their bright color and low cost, some unscrupulous merchants add Sudan dyes to food products such as chili powder and ketchup, resulting in serious health risks for consumers (Zheng, Mao, Zhu, Fu, Zare, & Karimi, 2022). When consumed, the remaining Sudan dyes in the human body can be converted into toxic primary aniline metabolites, which are known to be carcinogenic and genotoxic. Hence, Sudan dyes are strictly prohibited from being used as additives in any food product owing to their carcinogenicity and genotoxicity caused by the toxic primary metabolite aniline, which has been categorized as a class-Ⅲ carcinogen by the International Agency for Research on Cancer (Dong, Wu, Long, Wang, & Yu, 2021; Reile, Rodriguez, Fernandes, Gomes, Diniz, & Di Anibal, 2020). Therefore, it is crucial to rapidly and sensitively detect Sudan dyes, particularly in food products, to safeguard the health and rights of consumers.

To date, multitudinous methods, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (Dong et al., 2021; Li, Huang, Huang, Tang, Zhang, & Meng, 2020), UHPLC-MS/MS (Arrizabalaga-Larranaga, Epigmenio-Chamu, Santos, & Moyano, 2021), electrochemical sensors (Lei, Zhao, Wang, Chen, Hu, & Chen, 2022; Shen et al., 2022), and surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) (Yao-Say Solomon Adade et al., 2022; Yao, Ouyang, Lv, Dai, & Zhu, 2021), have been developed for the quantitative detection of Sudan dyes. However, their complex sample preparation, time-consuming operations, and expensive equipment have limited their widespread application. Fluorescence detection has gained popularity for its innate sensitivity, cost-effectiveness, and user-friendly operation. Various fluorescent materials have been explored for Sudan dye detection, including PPV/PVA fibrous membranes (Wu, Sun, Miao, Wu, & Fan, 2018), acriflavine-immobilized eggshell membranes (Li, Wang, Bai, & Wang, 2017), upconversion nanoparticles (Fang, Long, Wu, Li, Zhang, & Yao, 2016), PEI-silver nanoclusters (Chen, Li, Gao, Qu, Li, & Luo, 2014), Mn-ZnS quantum dots (Zhou et al., 2013), etc. However, the disadvantages of the above materials with complicated preparation processes, high cost, or high toxicity limit their further application. Recently, carbon dots（CDs）have evolved into a material with very promising applications for Sudan dye detection, owing to their outstanding optical properties and biocompatibility, low toxicity, cost-effectiveness, and high sensitivity. (Anmei, Qingmei, Yuye, & Yilin, 2018; Hu & Gao, 2020; Sun, Yan, Xu, Zhang, & Chen, 2021; Wu et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2020; Zhao, Zhang, Liu, Zhang, & Xiao, 2021). However, the fluorescence intensity of CDs may vary with the change in pH of the solution, influenced by the protonation or deprotonation of surface groups in acidic or alkaline conditions. Additionally, CDs can undergo aggregation-induced quenching at higher concentrations, limiting their application. (Yang et al., 2022).

Previous studies have demonstrated that incorporating CDs with porous materials can significantly improve the fluorescence stability of CDs. The incorporation of CDs into porous materials not only resolves practical challenges associated with CDs, but also imparts new functions to composite materials through synergistic effects, thereby expanding the applications of CDs (Chen, Xiao, Duan, Wu, Zhao, & Gong, 2021). As a type of metal–organic framework (MOF), zeolitic imidazolate frameworks (ZIFs) have garnered significant research attention because of ultrahigh porosity, large specific surface area, ease of synthesis, outstanding chemical and thermal stability, and water resistance (Ma et al., 2018). By using ZIF-8 as a substrate material, certain limitations of CDs as a fluorescence probe can be eliminated, resulting in improved fluorescence stability, sensitivity and detection range of the established methods. In recent years, several types of CDs have been effectively encapsulated within ZIF-8. For instance, Yang et al. (Yang et al., 2022) reported the one-step in-situ synthesis of CDs@ZIF-8, demonstrating its utility as a sensitive fluorescent sensor for Cu2+ detection. Jalili et al. (Jalili, Irani-nezhad, Khataee, & Joo, 2021) designed a dual emissive gCDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 composite using ZIF-8 as the host, gCDs and AuNCs as the gest. The established sensor was successfully applied to detect cephalexin, achieving an impressive low limit of detection (LOD). However, in most reported references, fluorescent probes were synthesized, and then found to have recognition ability for a specific target, rather than being designed specifically for the target. Targeted synthesis of fluorescent probes based on the target substance may be more valuable for target substance detection and the establishment of analytical methods.

Currently, many reported fluorescence analysis methods using CDs as probes rely on fluorescence quenching mechanisms. The interaction between CDs and Sudan dyes typically causes fluorescence quenching through two mechanisms: the internal filtering effect (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) (Sun et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2021). Under these circumstances, the fluorescence spectrum of the CDs overlaps with the UV–visible absorption (UV–vis) spectrum of Sudan dyes, enabling efficient energy transfer. Therefore, in this work, a fluorescent probe for detecting Sudan dyes was designed and synthesized by overlapping the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Sudan I with the emission spectrum of the fluorescent probe.

In this manuscript, green-emitting CDs were selected and prepared to interact with Sudan dyes for the purpose of detecting them. The selection of CDs was based on the overlap between the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Sudan I and the fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs, so energy transfer should be more likely to occur. Subsequently, we successfully prepared a fluorescent probe, CDs@ZIF-8, for ultrasensitive detection of Sudan dyes through a one-step, in-situ synthesis method by encapsulating CDs into ZIF-8. The experiments showed that when detecting Sudan I, the CDs@ZIF-8 fluorescent probe exhibited a 3.32-fold increase in sensitivity and a broader concentration range for detection compared to CDs. This method introduces an innovative approach for enhancing the sensitivity of fluorescent detection methods. Moreover, CDs@ZIF-8 exhibits superior pH resistance compared to CDs. Finally, we successfully utilized the CDs@ZIF-8 probe for Sudan I detection in paprika, achieving satisfactory recoveries. Overall, our results highlight the capacity of CDs@ZIF-8 as an extremely sensitive and reliable approach for detecting Sudan dyes in paprika.

Citric acid monohydrate, zinc nitrate hexahydrate, sodium citrate, vitamin C, and vitamin B1 were purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute. 2-Methylimidazole was obtained from Aladdin Reagents Co., Ltd. Urea, glucose, sucrose, hydrochloric acid, sodium hydroxide, magnesium sulfate, barium chloride, and aluminium chloride were obtained from Beijing Chemical Works. Representative amino acids (glutamic acid, lysine, cysteine, and histidine), organic solvents (methanol and ethanol), and Sudan dyes were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd. Throughout the experiment, deionized water was used, and all reagents used were of analytical grade and employed without the need for additional purification. A stock solution of Sudan Ⅰ at a concentration of 500 μM was formulated with anhydrous ethanol and stored at room temperature.

CDs were obtained by a laboratory Galanz microwave oven (P70F20CN3P-SR (W0)). The TEM images of the prepared materials were examined by a JEM-2100 field-emission transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). We collected powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns with an Empyrean diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (PANalytical B.V., Netherlands) in the 2θ range of 5-40°. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was carried out on a Fisher Nicolet iS5 spectrophotometer (Thermo, USA). We conducted thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) on the samples in a nitrogen atmosphere, spanning a temperature range from 25 to 800 °C, using a TA 2050 thermal analyser instrument (TA, USA) with a heating rate of 10 °C/min. Additionally, adsorption–desorption isotherms were analysed using a Micromeritics Autochem II 2920 analyser (Micromeritics, USA). We recorded UV–visible absorption spectra using a UV-2550 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were measured with an F-2700 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) equipped with a 1 × 1 cm2 quartz cell. Fluorescence lifetimes were measured using an FLS 920 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh, UK).

To obtain the overlap between the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Sudan I and the fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs, green-emitting CDs were selected and fabricated via a microwave-assisted method utilizing citric acid and urea as precursors (Thakur & Dan, 2021). In detail, in a 100 mL beaker, 0.55 g of citric acid monohydrate and 5 g of urea were dispersed in 10 mL of deionized water. After being sonicated for 5 min, the beaker was then heated in a 700 W microwave oven for 3 min, resulting in the transformation of the transparent solution into a dark green solid. Subsequently, the sample was allowed to cool to room temperature, and the resulting solid was dispersed in 15 mL of deionized water, followed by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. Finally, the green supernatant was collected and subjected to vacuum oven drying at 80 ℃ to obtain the desired CDs.

CDs@ZIF-8 was synthesized by incorporating the above-prepared CDs into the ZIF-8 preparation process. 2-Methylimidazole (0.6489 g) and Zn (NO3)2⋅6H2O (0.2933 g) were dissolved in 20 mL of anhydrous methanol and kept at room temperature. Then 400 μL CDs (5 mg/mL) was added to the solution of 2-methylimidazole with magnetic stirring. Next, the previously prepared Zn(NO3)2⋅6H2O solution was introduced into the mixture. The resulting mixture was stirred at room temperature for 24 h. The resulting CDs@ZIF-8 was collected through centrifugation at 12000 rpm for 5 min, followed by multiple washes with methanol until the washing supernatant exhibited no fluorescence. Subsequently, the product was dried overnight under vacuum (60 ℃) to obtain a pale-yellow powder. Pure white ZIF-8 was synthesized by a similar method without the addition of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Sudan dyes with CDs@ZIF-8
ce:section.ce:para: The previously synthesized CDs and CDs@ZIF-8 were utilized as fluorescent probes to identify Sudan I, a representative Sudan dye separately. The experimental procedure was as follows: 77.5 mg of CDs@ZIF-8 was dispersed in 31 mL of water, followed by 5 min of sonication. To explore the response of CDs@ZIF-8 to Sudan I, 1 mL of the previously mentioned CDs@ZIF-8 solution (2.5 mg/mL) was introduced into 5 mL centrifuge tubes containing varying concentrations (0.25–70 μM) of Sudan Ⅰ stock solution. The resulting mixture was then diluted to a final volume of 2 mL with anhydrous ethanol and incubated for 3 min before fluorescence detection. For the fluorescence spectra analysis, the excitation and emission slit widths were both configured at 5 nm, with the photomultiplier voltage adjusted to 700 V. The solution was excited at 410 nm, followed by recording emission spectra across the wavelength range from 430 to 750 nm. When detecting Sudan I using CDs as the fluorescent probe, 1 mL of a CD (2.6 mg/L) solution was added instead of CDs@ZIF-8.

The evaluation of CDs@ZIF-8′s selectivity for Sudan dyes was conducted by introducing potential interfering substances commonly found in food, such as sugars, vitamins, typical metal ions, and representative amino acids. Both the interfering substances and Sudan dyes were present at concentrations of 100 μM and 10 μM, respectively. To investigate the anti-interference of the detection system, Sudan I was used as an example. A solution containing CDs@ZIF-8 and 10 μM Sudan Ⅰ was prepared, and the interference substances were subsequently added to the CDs@ZIF-8-Sudan Ⅰ system for further study of fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Sudan I in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: Sudan I was detected in the paprika samples. The samples were pretreated as follows (Sun et al., 2021). Each 0.5 g sample was dispersed in 30 mL of ethanol, subjected to 25 min of sonication, and subsequently centrifuged for 10 min to collect the supernatant. The collected supernatant was filtered using both 0.45 μm and 0.22 μm filter membranes. The resulting filtrate was used for further determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization for the synthesis of CDs@ZIF-8
ce:section.ce:para:
The optimization of the amounts of CDs added to the synthesis process of CDs@ZIF-8 (as described in Section 2.4, “Preparation of CDs@ZIF-8”) was carried out through a series of experiments. Different amounts of CDs (1–5 mg) were separately added to the system during the preparation process to obtain CDs@ZIF-8. The introduction of Sudan I into the system resulted in the quenching of CDs@ZIF-8 fluorescence and the quenching effect (F0/F) was used as the evaluation standard for the optimum amounts of CDs to be incorporated. As depicted in Fig. S1, an increase in F0/F by approximately 1.26-fold was observed upon increasing the addition of CDs from 1 to 2 mg. However, a further increase in the amount of CDs (3–5 mg) resulted in a gradual decrease in F0/F. Therefore, the optimal amount for synthesizing CDs@ZIF-8 was determined to be 2 mg of CDs. The possible reasons for this phenomenon can be deduced as follows. When fewer CDs are added, there are not enough CDs to interact with Sudan I. Additionally, some of the CDs possess particle sizes smaller than 2 nm (approximately 1.5 nm, as shown in Fig. 2
A), which is similar to the channel sizes of ZIF-8 (approximately 0.6–1.5 nm, as shown in Fig. 3 F). With an increase in the number of CDs, small-sized CDs that are not embedded in ZIF-8 may block or occupy the ZIF-8 channels, leading to a decrease in the quenching efficiency of Sudan I. By adding 2 mg of CDs, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@ZIF-8 satisfies the requirements for detecting Sudan I while avoiding excessive small-sized CDs from blocking or entering the ZIF-8 channels.

Fig. 1 illustrates the schematic diagram depicting the synthesis of CDs@ZIF-8. To confirm the successful preparation of CDs@ZIF-8, TEM was utilized to analyse the sizes and morphologies of CDs, ZIF-8, and CDs@ZIF-8. Fig. 2A reveals that CDs were uniformly dispersed, appearing nearly spherical with an average size of 2.6 nm. Fig. 2B and 2C reveal the presence of distinct rhombic dodecahedral structures in both ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8, with average diameters of 42.0 nm and 47.0 nm, respectively. As depicted in Fig. 2D, CDs are uniformly distributed within the ZIF-8 frame structure. The results confirmed that CDs were successfully embedded in ZIF-8, as evidenced by the unchanged morphology and size of ZIF-8 after the addition of CDs.

FT-IR spectroscopy analysis was conducted to study the functional groups present in CDs, ZIF-8, and CDs@ZIF-8. As displayed in Fig. 3A, in the FT-IR spectrum of CDs, the absorption band observed between 3206 and 3438 cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibration of –OH and –NH2, and the characteristic peaks at 1068 and 1714 cm−1 were attributed to CO and CO, respectively, while peaks at 1181 and 1605 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of COC and CC. The characteristic peaks at 1408 and 3028 cm−1 were assigned to the bending vibration of –CH2 and the stretching vibration of CH. The results provide evidence for the presence of abundant amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs.

Regarding ZIF-8, the peaks at 3634 and 2929 cm−1 resulted in the stretching vibration of NH and CH, while the adsorption peaks at 1455 and 1420 cm−1, 755 and 699 cm−1 were resulted in the bending vibration inside and outside of the NH plane, illustrating the existence of a substantial number of amino groups on the surface of ZIF-8. The peak at 1578 cm−1 originated from the stretching vibration of CC, the peaks at 1307, 1140, and 992 cm−1 were linked to the stretching vibration of CN, and the adsorption peak at 420 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of Zn-N, confirming the successful synthesis of ZIF-8.

The crystal structures of ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8 were investigated through XRD experiments. It can be seen from Fig. 3B that the XRD patterns of both ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8 closely resembled the simulated ZIF-8 crystal structure, with a clear (110) peak reflection at 2θ = 7.3°, confirming the rhombic dodecahedral geometry of ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8 (Xu, Fang, Liu, Pan, Wang, & Wang, 2016). However, in the XRD pattern of CDs@ZIF-8, no distinct diffraction peak characteristic of CDs was observed. The possible reason is that the diffraction peak is too weak for clear observation due to the small particle size and the small content of CDs (Lin, Gao, Zheng, Chi, & Chen, 2014). This suggests that the quantity of embedded CDs in CDs@ZIF-8 is not substantial enough to influence the crystal lattice structure of ZIF-8. The above results demonstrate that the incorporation of CDs did not affect the growth and integrity of the ZIF-8 crystal, aligning with the FT-IR results.

Thermogravimetric analysis was conducted to investigate the thermal stability of ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8 in N2 flow. According to Fig. 3C, the TGA curves of ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8 exhibited two phases with mass loss. During the first phase between 160 ℃ and 600 ℃, both ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8 undergo a 9 % mass reduction in their initial mass, mainly resulting in the evaporation of residual solvents within the samples. However, there is a noticeable difference in the weight loss behavior between the two samples; CDs@ZIF-8 experiences a more rapid decrease in weight with increasing temperature than ZIF-8. This could be assigned to the decomposition of amino groups, oxygen-containing groups, and carbon nuclei present in the CD component of CDs@ZIF-8 (Thakur et al., 2021). In the second phrase, the mass loss became more significant with increasing temperature, which was primarily attributed to the thermal decomposition of the ZIF-8 framework. Within the temperature range of 600 ℃ − 800 ℃, both ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8 displayed comparable weight loss. At 800 ℃, CDs@ZIF-8 retained 55 % of its initial mass, confirming the outstanding thermal stability of both ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8. This observation further validates the successful synthesis of CDs@ZIF-8.

To verify the successful encapsulation of CDs within ZIF-8, an N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm measurement was conducted to explore the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific area and pore sizes of the CDs and CDs@ZIF-8. The results, presented in Fig. 3D and 3E, reveal that both ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8 displayed characteristics of type-I microporous materials. The specific surface area of ZIF-8 was measured at 1212.09 m2/g, while that of CDs@ZIF-8 was 1176 m2/g. The slightly lower specific surface area of CDs@ZIF-8 compared to that of ZIF-8 confirms the successful encapsulation of CDs within ZIF-8, which resulted in a slight decrease in the BET surface area. Moreover, Fig. 3F shows the pore size distributions of CDs@ZIF-8 and ZIF-8, which were found to be quite similar. This suggests that the introduced CDs were enveloped by the grown ZIF-8. This suggests that the sizes of CDs are too large to occupy the channels of ZIF-8 (He et al., 2014). Based on these observations, it can be concluded that CDs have been effectively encapsulated within ZIF-8.

The fluorescence properties of the as-synthesized CDs@ZIF-8 were investigated. When placed under natural lighting conditions, the ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8 powders exhibited white and pale-yellow appearances, respectively. This phenomenon was consistent when the powders were evenly dispersed in water. When excited by 365 nm ultraviolet light, the CDs@ZIF-8 solution emitted bright green fluorescence, while ZIF-8 showed no fluorescence (Fig. S2a). These results indicated that the superior fluorescence observed in the CDs@ZIF-8 solution can be attributed to the presence of CDs, providing a practical basis for using it as a fluorescent probe for detecting Sudan dyes.

The fluorescence spectra of CDs@ZIF-8, recorded when excited at different excitation wavelengths, are presented in Fig. S2b. The emission and excitation peaks for CDs@ZIF-8 were located at 518 nm and 410 nm, respectively. Interestingly, as displayed in Fig. S2c, the emission spectrum of CDs@ZIF-8 exhibited a blueshift (530 to 518 nm) when excited at 410 nm compared to CDs alone. This shift is due to coordination-induced emission (CIE), which occurs during the coordination of CDs and dimethylimidazole ligands with zinc ions to form CDs@ZIF-8. The rigid network structures of CDs had to adapt and undergo twisting to accommodate the rigid structure of CDs@ZIF-8, resulting in a blueshift (Fu et al., 2022). Moreover, ZIF-8 showed almost no fluorescence at 518 nm, further confirming that the fluorescence observed in CDs@ZIF-8 was derived from CDs and not from ZIF-8. This result underscores the successful preparation of CDs@ZIF-8.

Furthermore, during the preparation of CDs@ZIF-8, the obtained CDs@ZIF-8 solid was washed several times until no fluorescence occurred in the washing solution, suggesting that CDs were encapsulated within ZIF-8 rather than being physically adsorbed onto its surface.

The impact of Sudan Ⅰ on the fluorescence of CDs and CDs@ZIF-8 systems was investigated using CDs and CDs@ZIF-8 as fluorescent probes. 1 Sudan Ⅰ (10 μM) was added to the CDs and CDs@ZIF-8 solutions. The fluorescence intensities of both CDs and CDs@ZIF-8 decreased with the introduction of Sudan I (Fig. S2d). Notably, the fluorescence quenching effect observed in CDs@ZIF-8 was determined to be greater than that of CDs, primarily due to the robust adsorption and accumulation of ZIF-8. This observation suggests that the presence of ZIF-8 enhances the sensitivity of detection.

The stability of CDs@ZIF-8 was investigated, and the results are presented in Fig. S3a. The fluorescence intensity of CDs remained stable under acidic condition, but gradually decreased with increasing of pH under alkaline conditions. However, CDs@ZIF-8 maintained similar fluorescence intensities across the pH range of 2–12, indicating its excellent stability against pH interference. This can be attributed to the protective effect of ZIF-8, which is stable under alkaline conditions and hence safeguards the CDs embedded within it. Fig. S3b and Fig. S3c demonstrate that the fluorescence intensity of CDs@ZIF-8 remained virtually unchanged when stored at room temperature for 7 d and exposed to UV light for 60 min, respectively. These results indicate the remarkable fluorescence stability and photobleaching resistance of CDs@ZIF-8.

To assess the stability of ZIF-8 during the sample testing process, an examination of the morphology and crystal structure of CDs@ZIF-8 was conducted before and after sample detection. The morphology of CDs@ZIF-8 before and after detection of the actual sample was characterized by SEM. It can be seen from Fig. S4 that the morphology of CDs@ZIF-8 has basically not changed before and after the actual sample detection, still retaining its rhombic dodecahedron shape.

The crystal structure of CDs@ZIF-8 was examined using XRD both before and after practical sample detection. As shown in Fig. S5, the XRD patterns of CDs@ZIF-8 before and after sample testing exhibited striking similarities, with a distinct (110) peak reflection at 2θ = 7.3, confirming the rhombic dodecahedron geometry of ZIF-8. However, following actual sample testing, a small peak at 2θ = 11° was observed in CDs@ZIF-8, indicating the formation of a new complex due to the adsorption effect of ZIF-8, resulting in the adsorption of Sudan I.(Zhou, Li, Owens, & Chen, 2019).

To assess the selectivity of CDs@ZIF-8 for Sudan dye detection, the fluorescence response of the probe to various potential interfering substances, including sucrose, glucose, sodium citrate, VC, VB1, citric acid, urea, representative amino acids, and metal ions, was studied. As displayed in Fig. S6a, only Sudan dyes resulted in a significant quenching effect, while the others had no noticeable impact on CDs@ZIF-8. This suggests that CDs@ZIF-8 exhibits good selectivity when detecting Sudan dyes. To further assess the anti-interference capability of CDs@ZIF-8, we selected Sudan I (10 μM) as a representative and added possible interfering substances (100 μM) for analysis. The results presented in Fig. S6b show that the addition of interfering substances did not cause any significant changes in the fluorescence intensity. Therefore, it can be inferred that CDs@ZIF-8 exhibits excellent anti-interference ability when detecting Sudan dyes.

To achieve excellent sensing performance for the detection of Sudan Ⅰ, various factors affecting the quenching efficiency of the fluorescent probe were optimized, including the concentration of CDs@ZIF-8, pH, and reaction time. Detailed experimental procedures and results are shown in the Electronic Supplementary Material (Fig. S7). The optimal conditions for detecting Sudan I were found to be 1.25 mg/mL CDs@ZIF-8, pH = 7, and a reaction time of 2 min. Fig. S8a and Fig. S8b display that the fluorescence intensities of the systems (CDs@ZIF-8 and CDs as probes, separately) decreased progressively along with the increasing concentration of Sudan I. The quenching efficiencies F0/F and the concentrations of Sudan Ⅰ were fitted under the traditional Stern-Volmer equation: F0/F = Ksv[C] + 1. Here, F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of the probe when there is no Sudan I and when Sudan I is present, respectively. Ksv and [C] represent the quenching constant and concentration of Sudan Ⅰ, respectively.

As displayed in Fig. S8c, there exists a linear correlation between F0/F for the CDs@ZIF-8 system within the concentration range spanning from 0.25 to 70 μM and for the CDs system within the range of 0.75–50 μM. The Stern-Volmer equations are F0/F = 4.64 × 104 × [C] + 1 and F0/F = 3.24 × 104 × [C] + 1 respectively, with linear correlation coefficients R2 of 0.996 and 0.992, respectively. The Ksv values were determined to be 4.64 × 104 and 3.24 × 104 L/mol, respectively, indicating that CDs@ZIF-8 has higher sensitivity and a wider detection range for Sudan Ⅰ. The LODs were 76.56 nM and 0.25 μM, respectively. Compared with CDs as probes, the detection range was wider and the LOD of the CDs@ZIF-8 probe was 3.32 times that of the CDs probe. The results demonstrate that CDs@ZIF-8 shows better sensitivity than CDs, mainly attributed to the porous structure of ZIF-8 that prevents the aggregation of CDs encapsulated in it and increases the contact area between CDs and Sudan Ⅰ, thereby improving the sensitivity.

Additionally, an analysis for detecting Sudan II, Sudan III, and Sudan IV was also carried out under the optimized conditions for testing Sudan I. As shown in Fig. S9, the linear ranges of detection were 0.2–40 μM, 0.1–25 μM and 0.15–10 μM, respectively, with limits of detection of 69.43 nM, 34.14 nM and 47.48 nM, respectively. These results indicate that CDs@ZIF-8 is highly sensitive to Sudan dyes. Compared with some reported research methods (Table S1), the detection method using CDs@ZIF-8 as probes for Sudan I is more efficient and sensitive.

The mechanism responsible for the observed fluorescence quenching in the CDs@ZIF-8 fluorescent probe caused by the presence of Sudan I was investigated. Fig. 4 A shows that no new absorption peak appeared when Sudan Ⅰ was added to CDs@ZIF-8. This suggests that there was no formation of a nonluminescent ground-state complex between Sudan I and CDs@ZIF-8, thus ruling out the possibility of a static quenching mechanism (Sun et al., 2021). Fig. 4B indicates a substantial overlap between the absorption spectrum of Sudan Ⅰ and the excitation and emission spectra of CDs@ZIF-8, confirming that Sudan Ⅰ can effectively absorb the emitted light from CDs@ZIF-8. According to the preliminary inference, fluorescence quenching may be due to FRET or IFE effects. (Han et al., 2020; Hu, Gao, & Luo, 2021). Typically, for FRET, the fluorescence lifetime of the fluorescent probe is shortened upon introducing a target, while for IFE, the fluorescence lifetime is unchanged. (Korah, Chacko, Mathew, John, Abraham, & Mathew, 2022). To determine which mechanism is responsible for the observed fluorescence quenching, the fluorescence lifetimes of CDs@ZIF-8 were measured in both the absence and presence of Sudan I, and the results are presented in Fig. 4C. The fluorescence lifetime was approximately the same before and after adding Sudan I, at 12.05 ns and 11.72 ns, respectively. This finding rules out the possibility of FRET and suggests a result of the IFE for the fluorescence quenching. To further confirm the fluorescence quenching caused by internal filtration effect, the excitation wavelength-dependent quenching spectrum compared with the absorption spectrum of Sudan I was investigated, which is shown in Fig. S10. The similar spectral shapes confirm that the fluorescence quenching of CDs@ZIF-8 in this study was caused by Sudan I absorbing the fluorescence of CDs@ZIF-8, which is known as IFE. (Li et al., 2020).

In addition, due to the Stern-Volmer equation F0/F = 4.64 × 104 × [C] + 1 obtained in the experiment (shown in Section 3.5 Sensing measurements), it is evident that there is a highly linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and the concentration of Sudan I. This indicates that the fluorescence quenching of CDs@ZIF-8 induced by Sudan I is a static quenching process.(Han et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2023; Zu et al., 2017).
To evaluate the potential of utilizing CDs@ZIF-8 as a fluorescent probe for Sudan I detection in paprika, three actual paprika samples purchased from a local supermarket were subjected to analysis.

To examine the influence of the matrix effect of real samples on the detection of Sudan I, a calibration curve was prepared in a solution of the paprika sample. The fluorescence spectra and the calibration curve are displayed in Fig. 5 . The results demonstrate that in the paprika matrix solution, when utilizing CDs@ZIF-8 as a probe and applying the Stern-Volmer equation (F0/F = Ksv [C] + 1), the Ksv value was determined to be 2.8 × 104 L/mol, with the linear regression equation F0/F = 2.8 × 104 × [C] + 1. The detection range for Sudan I in the paprika sample was found to be 1–50 µM (0.99–49.66 mg/kg). The LOD and LOQ were determined to be 0.28 µM (0.28 mg/kg) and 0.94 µM (0.93 mg/kg), respectively. These values differ to some extent from the detection parameters obtained in pure solutions, demonstrating the existence of a matrix effect in actual samples. However, the results also show that the quantification of Sudan I in actual samples can be achieved through the establishment of matrix-matched calibration curves. Therefore, quantification of Sudan I in the samples was conducted.

As Sudan I was not detected in the samples, a spiked recovery experiment was conducted by adding known amounts of Sudan I to the samples. The analysis results for Sudan I in the actual samples, utilizing CDs as probes, are presented in Table 1 , indicating that the recovery rate of Sudan I detected by this method was between 96.1 % and 104.6 %. The relative standard deviations (RSD) ranged from 0.3 % to 5.9 % across the analysis. These results suggest the promising potential of CDs@ZIF-8 for detecting Sudan Ⅰ in actual samples.

To further evaluate the repeatability and reproducibility of Sudan I detection in actual samples using CDs@ZIF-8 as a probe, interday and intraday precision analyses were conducted. The data are presented in Table S2. The results show that, when detecting Sudan I in paprika, this method achieved average recoveries of 97.8 % to 107.4 % for intraday testing and 97.7 % to 104.2 % for interday testing, with an RSD below 6 %. These findings demonstrate the method's strong repeatability and reproducibility.

In this manuscript, a type of green emission CDs was selected and utilized as a fluorescent probe for Sudan I analysis. This was derived from the alignment between the UV–vis adsorption spectrum of Sudan I and the emission spectrum of CDs. Our experiments have demonstrated that embedding CDs in ZIF-8 can significantly enhance the pH resistance of CDs. Hence, CDs@ZIF-8 was selected to analyse Sudan I. Upon adding Sudan I to the system, quenching of the fluorescence intensities of both CDs and CDs@ZIF-8 was observed. This phenomenon was due to the IFE effect. The data showed that CDs@ZIF-8 exhibited a wider concentration range for detection (0.25–70 μM) and higher sensitivity (LOD: 76.56 nM, 3.32-fold increase) than CDs alone (concentration range: 0.75–50 μM; LOD: 0.25 μM). This can be attributed to the enriching ability provided by porous ZIF-8. Therefore, embedding CDs in ZIF-8 proves to be more conducive for target detection than using single CDs. A recovery experiment was conducted on Sudan I in actual samples, and the results were satisfactory. The LOD and LOQ were found to be 0.28 µM (0.28 mg/kg) and 0.94 µM (0.93 mg/kg) in the detection of 1–50 µM (0.99–49.66 mg/kg), respectively, with an RSD ranging from 0.3 % to 5.9 %. Although using the synthesized CDs@ZIF-8 as probes can only detect the presence of Sudan dyes in food but cannot specifically identify which type of Sudan dye is present, it is important to note that all Sudan dyes are prohibited additives in food, and the detection of any one type signifies the presence of illegal additives. Additionally, this issue can also be addressed through techniques such as molecular imprinting. Even if there is such imperfection, our design represents great research and practical application value for detecting target objects and provides a new strategy for the application of CDs under extreme conditions.

Yanjie Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Xiaofeng Wei: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Yu Su: Investigation, Methodology. Rui Xu: Investigation, Methodology. Daqian Song: Methodology. Lan Ding: Writing – review & editing. Yanhua Chen: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 201804049), Jilin Provincial Science and Technology Development Plan Project (20230402022GH), and The Open Funds of the State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry (SKLEAC202305).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zu, F. L., Yan, F. Y., Bai, Z. J., Xu, J. X., Wang, Y. Y., Huang, Y. C., & Zhou, X. G. (2017). The quenching of the fluorescence of carbon dots: A review on mechanisms and applications. Microchimica Acta, 184(7), 1899-1914. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00604-017-2318-9.
